data.title: Minerals Technologies outlines Q2 2026 EPS of $1.60-$1.65 while tracking about a 14% operating margin for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Minerals Technologies (MTX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong first quarter with broad-based double-digit growth, and we're seeing early proof that our strategic growth investments are paying off," said (Chairman & CEO Douglas Dietrich), as the company reported "first quarter sales came in at $547 million, up 11% from prior year" and reiterated, "we projected that these initiatives would drive $100 million in annualized revenue beginning this year" with Q1 delivering "the first portion of that growth."

  • On segment performance, (Chairman & CEO Douglas Dietrich) said, "we saw an 11% year-over-year increase in our Consumer and Specialties segment" and "our Engineered Solutions segment sales increased 12% over last year," and added, "operating income was $68 million, excluding special items" and "earnings per share were $1.38, up 21%."

  • Addressing the Middle East conflict, (Chairman & CEO Douglas Dietrich) said, "we've avoided any material impact on sales or operations to date" but "we have seen an impact ... with higher energy and freight costs," which the company is "addressing through pricing actions and temporary surcharges," while noting, "we typically produce our products within the same region or country where we sell them" and calling localization "one of MTI's key differentiators."

  • On growth projects, (Chairman & CEO Douglas Dietrich) said, "this is a record sales quarter for cat litter, which grew 19% over last year," and added, "we expect to have [the natural oil purification] fully online late in the second quarter" to support renewable fuels demand, while noting PFAS remediation momentum: "we're ... on track for 10 or possibly more new water utility implementations for our FLUORO-SORB ... product in the second half."

  • In prepared remarks, (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Erik Aldag) said, "Q1 sales were $547 million, up 5% sequentially" and "operating income was $68 million in the first quarter," adding that "pricing was relatively minimal" in Q1 and that the quarter absorbed "about $5 million" of higher costs versus guidance that had "assumed $2 million to $3 million."

Outlook

  • For full-year demand and growth, (Chairman & CEO Douglas Dietrich) said, "our current projection is for mid-single-digit sales growth in 2026, and this could inflect higher if the market strength we are currently seeing continues," while cautioning the company is "mindful of continued macro uncertainty, particularly around energy costs."

  • For Q2, (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Erik Aldag) guided that "we expect second quarter sales to be approximately $560 million" with "operating income for the quarter of approximately $80 million" and "earnings per share of between $1.60 and $1.65," while highlighting the cost environment: "our outlook for the second quarter includes $12 million of higher inflationary costs on a year-over-year basis" and "we are expecting around a $3 million temporary impact on our operating income in the second quarter."

  • On margin trajectory, (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Erik Aldag) said, "we're currently tracking to about a 14% operating margin for the full year" and "we're expecting margins to improve by more than 100 basis points from the first half to the second half, approaching our 15% run rate target in the second half."

Financial Results

  • (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Erik Aldag) said Q1 Consumer & Specialties sales were "$297 million" and Engineered Solutions sales were "$250 million," and added that Q1 cash generation improved: "first quarter cash from operations was $32 million, up $37 million from prior year," with "capital expenditures in the first quarter ... $23 million" and expected full-year capex of "$90 million to $100 million."

  • On segment profitability, (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Erik Aldag) said Consumer & Specialties operating income was "$33 million" and Engineered Solutions operating income was "$39 million, representing 15.7% of sales," while noting margins were pressured by "the rapid shift in freight and energy costs" and "higher corporate expense ... mark-to-market impact on stock-based compensation."

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities: How much growth was price vs. volume, and how did MTX grow vs. markets? (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Aldag) replied, "pricing was relatively minimal ... around 1%" and cited new business, including being "certainly outpacing market growth" in cat litter and seeing Environmental & Infrastructure show "a few consecutive quarters of growth."

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities: What visibility is there in Environmental & Infrastructure projects? (Group President of Engineered Solutions Brett Argirakis) said the pipeline "really has increased" with projects "pretty healthy really into the third quarter" and "we are getting more RFQs."

  • Michael Harrison, Seaport Research Partners: What drove Q1 growth (FX and extra days), and why wasn’t there more margin leverage? (Senior VP of Finance & Treasury and CFO Aldag) said FX was "about 3%" and "the extra days contributed to about 2% to 3%"; on margins, (CFO Aldag) pointed to soft residential construction and cost timing, and (Chairman & CEO Dietrich) said the margin impact was "unexpected" from "energy and freight" but "this is a temporary thing."

  • David Silver, Freedom Capital: Clarify FLUORO-SORB implementations and geography. (Chairman & CEO Dietrich) said, "these 10 are full installations," and (Group President Argirakis) said pilots are occurring in "EU, U.K., Japan and now Hong Kong," adding, "over the last 6 to 8 months, our requests to pilot FLUORO-SORB in the large surface water facilities has doubled."

  • David Silver, Freedom Capital: What does the PCC satellite pipeline look like and is packaging increasing? (Group President of Consumer & Specialties D. J. Monagle) said the pipeline is "another 2 dozen opportunities" and packaging has "been migrating more towards 25%, 30% is packaging," with technology mix "50% ... standard PCC" and "the other 50% ... NewYield and GCC."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but probing on margin flow-through and cost lags, including: "I'm just a little bit surprised" (Michael Harrison, Seaport Research Partners) and questions on "what could drive that to be better or worse" regarding the Q2 price/cost lag.

  • Management’s tone was confident on growth projects but cautious on costs, with (Chairman & CEO Dietrich) stating, "we've avoided any material impact" to date from geopolitical events while (CFO Aldag) emphasized timing: "we are anticipating a timing lag of up to 90 days" and expected to "fully offset" higher input costs.

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from describing 2025 as "a more challenging year" (Chairman & CEO Dietrich, Q4 2025) to highlighting Q1 as "a strong start to the year" (CFO Aldag, Q1 2026), while analysts continued to press on margins in both quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, (Chairman & CEO Dietrich) highlighted acceleration in growth investments, including "record sales quarter for cat litter" and FLUORO-SORB moving to "10 or possibly more" second-half implementations; in Q4 2025, management emphasized that new cat litter business would "begin to ramp up at the beginning of the second quarter" (Chairman & CEO Dietrich, Q4 2025) and that FLUORO-SORB had "10 new ... installations scheduled for this year" (Chairman & CEO Dietrich, Q4 2025).

  • Guidance framing evolved from Q4 2025’s view that the margin impact from higher costs "should be limited to the first quarter" (CFO Aldag, Q4 2025) to Q1 2026 acknowledging continued near-term pressure: "a $3 million temporary impact" in Q2 (CFO Aldag) tied to "contractual pricing arrangements."

Risks and Concerns

  • (Chairman & CEO Dietrich) cited "higher energy and freight costs" as "our biggest current challenges" and said price actions will take effect "over the next 90 days due to contractual terms," while (CFO Aldag) added Q2 includes "$12 million of higher inflationary costs" and the company expects a temporary lag impact.

  • End-market softness remained a recurring theme, with (Chairman & CEO Dietrich) stating, "One area where we've not seen much improvement is the North America residential construction market," and (CFO Aldag) noting residential construction "remains soft compared to prior years."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a volume-led growth quarter supported by strategic investments, while acknowledging a near-term earnings timing headwind from energy and freight inflation that is expected to lag through contractual pricing. For Q2, the company guided to approximately $560 million in sales and EPS of $1.60 to $1.65, with a temporary $3 million operating income impact from price/cost lag, while continuing to track to about a 14% operating margin for 2026 and targeting a return toward a 15% run-rate in the second half as pricing actions and growth-project ramps take hold.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Camden outlines 2026 core FFO midpoint of $6.75 as it expects Q2 core FFO of $1.65-$1.69
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Camden Property Trust (CPT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We recently announced some important changes to Camden's executive team with the promotions of Alex Jessett, Laurie Baker and Ben Fraker... [featuring] Camden's homegrown talent.” (Executive Chairman of the Board Richard Campo)
  • “As Ben will cover in detail, we had a strong first quarter. Much of the outperformance was timing related.” (CEO & Trust Manager Alexander Jessett)
  • “In the first quarter, we recorded our lowest bad debt level since the onset of COVID-19 at less than 40 basis points.” (CEO Jessett)
  • “Our California sales process is progressing on schedule... we are currently in the diligence process with one buyer for the entire portfolio with an anticipated close date at the end of June or early July.” (CEO Jessett)
  • “We continue to assume approximately 60% of the sales proceeds will be reinvested through 1031 exchanges into our existing high-demand, high-growth Sunbelt markets.” (CEO Jessett)
  • “After quarter end, we acquired Camden Alpharetta... and Camden at Lake Nona... for a combined $170 million.” (CEO Jessett)
  • “Our first quarter 2026 annualized net turnover rate of 30% was one of the lowest in our company's history.” (President & COO Laurie Baker)
  • “We proactively recast our $1.2 billion unsecured revolving line of credit... lowering all-in pricing by 15 basis points.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Benjamin Fraker)
  • “We also issued $600 million of 10-year unsecured bonds at an all-in effective rate of 5%.” (CFO Fraker)

Outlook

  • “We are reaffirming the midpoint of our full year same-store revenue guidance at 0.75%... reaffirming the midpoint of our same-store expense guidance at 3%... [and] same-store NOI guidance remains unchanged at negative 0.5%.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “We are keeping the midpoint of our full year core FFO per share guidance of $6.75.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “We expect core FFO per share for the second quarter to be within the range of $1.65 to $1.69.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “This anticipated decline is driven by a $0.04 sequential decrease in same-store NOI... partially offset by $0.01 of additional non-same-store NOI from our completed and projected net acquisitions.” (CFO Fraker)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that “we expect operating conditions will improve over the course of the year with modest acceleration in the second half of 2026,” management maintained full-year core FFO midpoint guidance and reiterated the setup for a stronger back half. (Executive Vice Chairman D. Keith Oden, Q4 2025; CFO Fraker, Q1 2026)

Financial Results

  • “For the first quarter, core FFO was $1.70 per share.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “The outperformance compared to guidance was driven by $0.01 from higher revenues... primarily attributable to lower-than-anticipated bad debt and higher collections on delinquent rent.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “Another $0.02 resulted from property expense savings, which were largely timing related.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “The remaining $0.01... was due to the timing of third-party construction fee income.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “We recorded $58.2 million of noncore FFO charges, most of which were related to the previously disclosed $53 million class action lawsuit settlement.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “The remaining charges were primarily due to $4.9 million of anticipated investment losses from 2 climate technology funds.” (CFO Fraker)

Q&A

  • Eric Wolfe, Citigroup: Asked about the expected “pretty big ramp” in blended lease rate growth; CEO Jessett: “April occupancy is right around 95.4%... blended rates... up about 100 basis points in April... we're anticipating... a pretty strong third quarter... [and] a better fourth quarter... because you've got supply coming down so dramatically.”
  • James Feldman, Wells Fargo: Asked how concessions are trending; CEO Jessett: “we don't offer concessions... we are seeing concessions come down fairly meaningful in most of our markets.”
  • Austin Wurschmidt, KeyBanc: Asked about renewal asking vs. achieved; President Baker: “we're feeling pretty good about kind of landing right around the same range of usually 50 bps on where people sign.”
  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Asked whether larger scale would improve data; CEO Jessett: “bigger is not better, better is better... we've got enough information... to make the appropriate decisions.”
  • Jana Galan, BofA Securities: Asked about acquisition cap rates and lease-ups; management: “cap rates have really been stable... newer, well-located properties in the Sunbelt... in the 4.5% to 5% range.” (Unidentified executive)
  • Haendel St. Juste, Mizuho: Asked about incremental buybacks and 1031/tax limits; CFO Fraker: “we bought back $693 million... [and] as of now... no additional share repurchases in our 2026 guidance,” while CEO Jessett added: “that does not mean that we will not do any additional share repurchases.”
  • John Kim, BMO: Asked why sell Southern California to one buyer and why not buy more pre-close; CEO Jessett: “we have limited our execution risk while maximizing proceeds... we've actually been awarded another $250 million worth of acquisitions.”
  • Alexander Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: Asked whether the softness is jobs or supply; CEO Jessett: “This is entirely a supply story... merely a matter of absorbing the existing supply that's out there.”
  • Ami Probandt, UBS: Asked about Houston weakness and gas prices; CEO Jessett: “Houston's consumer sentiment has fallen pretty dramatically... Supply has come down pretty dramatically, Houston will get better. It's just a sentiment issue,” and Executive Chairman Campo added: “the consumer is actually doing really well... [but] that tension... is making them slower... to make housing decisions.”
  • Richard Hightower, Barclays: Asked what changed in revenue management post-RealPage; CEO Jessett: “it's not in the rearview mirror yet,” and “we do not think that any of those changes will have any negative impact on us whatsoever.”
  • Unknown Analyst, Goldman Sachs: Asked if tax refunds drove April improvement; CEO Jessett: “I don't think that's the driver... I think the driver... is us hitting the typical leasing season and the continued absorption of supply.”
  • Alex Kim, Zelman: Asked how development ranks vs. acquisitions/buybacks and Denver “Camden Baker”; CEO Jessett: “number one is share repurchases... developments and acquisitions are sort of a toss up... you can buy real estate at a discount to replacement costs,” and on Baker: “the math isn't that great... if we don't [see improvements], we won't.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing, probing the “pretty big ramp,” Houston underperformance, post-RealPage pricing changes, and the cadence of recovery. (Eric Wolfe, Citigroup; Ami Probandt, UBS; Richard Hightower, Barclays)
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, using phrases such as “we are feeling pretty good,” “remain confident,” and “This is entirely a supply story,” while also qualifying timing around the California sale and 1031 exchanges. (CEO Jessett; CFO Fraker)
  • Versus the prior quarter’s repeated “uncertainty” framing, the current call leaned more into “change” and operational “green shoots,” while maintaining the same full-year core FFO midpoint. (Executive Chairman Campo, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026; CFO Fraker)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Leadership presentation shifted from “Chairman of the Board & CEO” to Executive Chairman plus a “new CEO,” with Campo saying the promotions “will ensure the continuity of Camden's family values.” (Executive Chairman Campo)
  • The California sale moved from broad marketing and value “range from $1.5 billion to $2 billion” to a single-buyer diligence track with “anticipated close date at the end of June or early July,” while maintaining the reinvestment and buyback framework. (President & CFO Jessett, Q4 2025; CEO Jessett, Q1 2026)
  • Guidance posture was steady quarter-over-quarter, with CFO Fraker reiterating the “midpoint... $6.75” and reaffirming same-store midpoints despite Q1 timing-related outperformance. (CFO Fraker)
  • Analyst focus stayed centered on rent recovery timing, supply absorption, and capital allocation (buybacks vs. acquisitions), with incremental attention on Houston’s softness and revenue management practices. (Multiple analysts; CEO Jessett; CFO Fraker)

Risks and Concerns

  • “The timing of the exchanges can add considerable variability to our 2026 earnings as we do not receive the sales proceeds until we complete the exchanges.” (CEO Jessett)
  • “We believe it is premature to extrapolate one quarter's performance into a full year trend, particularly given market variability.” (CFO Fraker)
  • “All of that litigation... it's not in the rearview mirror yet.” (CEO Jessett)
  • “Houston's consumer sentiment has fallen pretty dramatically in '26 as compared to '25.” (CEO Jessett)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as “a strong first quarter” but “timing related,” reaffirmed full-year 2026 core FFO midpoint guidance of $6.75, and positioned the year around peak leasing season and supply absorption. Executives emphasized the expected midyear Southern California portfolio closing (end of June/early July target), ongoing 1031 redeployment into Sunbelt markets, and continued flexibility on buybacks beyond what is assumed in guidance, while highlighting improving April occupancy and early “green shoots” across several markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Imperial Oil targets Kearl 300,000 bpd and Cold Lake 165,000 bpd while renewing NCIB in June
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Imperial Oil (IMO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Since our last earnings call, we've seen significant volatility in commodity markets, driven by geopolitical events in the Middle East... resulting in a materially different outlook for this year and potentially beyond," said (Chairman, President & CEO John Whelan).
  • "We also continue to advance our business transformation restructuring plans... we expect to capture significant long-term efficiency and effectiveness benefits as we further leverage rapidly advancing technology and ExxonMobil's global capability centers," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "Cash flows from operating activities were $756 million in the quarter. Excluding the impact of working capital, cash flows from operating activities were over $1.2 billion," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "We intend to renew our Normal Course Issuer Bid at the end of June," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "Starting with financial results for the first quarter, we recorded net income of $940 million... driven by higher incentive compensation charges... and unfavorable upstream realizations," said (Senior VP of Finance & Administration and Controller D. Lyons).
  • "In the first quarter, we paid $350 million of dividends... and we declared a second quarter dividend of $0.87 per share," said (Controller Lyons).

Outlook

  • "Our corporate strategy and investment plans remain consistent," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "We are -- remain very focused on Kearl getting it to 300,000, Cold Lake getting it to 165,000 barrels per day... and of course, we're advancing our EBRT pilot," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "You shouldn't expect or anticipate major changes. We weren't waiting for a price signal to drive pace," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "We have Mahihkan, which we've started to invest in, and that's still on track to bring on 30,000 barrels a day... starting up in 2029," said (CEO Whelan).

Financial Results

  • "We recorded net income of $940 million, down $348 million from the first quarter of 2025," said (Controller Lyons).
  • "The total [incentive compensation] charge in the quarter was $143 million after tax... driven by a historic share price increase of almost $65, over 50% in the quarter," said (Controller Lyons).
  • "Cash flows from operating activities for the first quarter were $1,239 million, down $521 million from the first quarter of '25," said (Controller Lyons).
  • "Cash flows from operating activities were also impacted by unfavorable deferred tax effects of about $350 million," said (Controller Lyons).
  • "Capital expenditures in the first quarter were $478 million, $80 million higher than the first quarter of 2025 and $173 million lower than the fourth quarter of 2025," said (Controller Lyons).

Q&A

  • Dennis Fong, CIBC Capital Markets: "can you talk about opportunities to maybe accelerate some of that pipeline of projects" at Cold Lake? CEO Whelan: "you shouldn't expect or anticipate major changes" and "Grand Rapids SA-SAGD is continuing to perform very well, above 20,000 barrels a day... Mahihkan... still on track to bring on 30,000 barrels a day... starting up in 2029."
  • Dennis Fong, CIBC Capital Markets: "provide... flexibility... and... opportunities to capitalize on dislocations... specifically focusing around distillates and jet fuel"? CEO Whelan: "we do opportunistically look at exporting" and (Vice President Scott Maloney) added, "we've been maximizing our production of diesel and jet molecules over gasoline."
  • Greg Pardy, RBC Capital Markets: "progress in terms of the restructuring" and "key benchmarks of success"? CEO Whelan: "we are advancing that transition on track today" and "we have had people leave the organization... about 130 people in the first quarter of this year... [and] that's going to continue pretty ratably."
  • Greg Pardy, RBC Capital Markets: does restructuring change execution capability for Aspen? CEO Whelan: "Absolutely. That's exactly the way to think about it... it doesn't change anything with our governance, doesn't change anything with our strategy."
  • Menno Hulshof, TD Cowen: status of Kearl initiatives "within enhanced bitumen recovery"? (Senior Vice President of Upstream Cheryl Gomez-Smith): "We've got two [recovery projects] in the hopper right now... KFCC... come online at the end of the year... [and] CST... in development."
  • Menno Hulshof, TD Cowen: "recent increase to the SCO premium" and duration? Maloney: "we feel like there's ample feedstocks" and CEO Whelan added, "we're not going to predict the future synthetic premium, but that may persist for a little bit."
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: return of capital and whether "an SIB" is possible given share price strength? Controller Lyons: "we said we're going to renew our NCIB" and "is there an SIB... [it] just going to depend on where cash goes."
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: what to accomplish in Strathcona and Kearl turnarounds? Controller Lyons: "Strathcona... crude unit... had a 10-year run" and Cheryl Gomez-Smith added, "some modifications on the primary separation cell... and... hardening on our surge bin."
  • Doug Leggate, Wolfe Research: royalties when WTI vs. WCS move differently? Controller Lyons: "the actual royalty payment is tied to your realizations on bitumen" and "for Kearl... we cap out at 9% gross."
  • Doug Leggate, Wolfe Research: SAGD technology ownership and international expansion? CEO Whelan: "Imperial has kind of been the center of excellence around heavy oil technology" and "we at Imperial are not looking to go outside of Canada."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, with detailed, multi-part questions on capital pacing, downstream optimization, restructuring execution, and return of capital, including, "how do you think about buying back stock here and the potential for an SIB" (Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs) and "can you walk us through how we should think about" royalties (Doug Leggate, Wolfe Research).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing consistency and confidence, including, "our corporate strategy and investment plans remain consistent" (CEO Whelan) and "we are advancing that transition on track today" (CEO Whelan).
  • Versus last quarter, Q1 language leaned more on volatility and geopolitics, with CEO Whelan saying, "we've seen significant volatility in commodity markets," compared with Q4’s framing of "another strong quarter" and large identified items.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 centered on commodity volatility, turnarounds, and implementation of restructuring, while Q4 highlighted specific one-time items and asset decisions, including "cease production at our Norman Wells asset... by the end of the third quarter of 2026" (CEO Whelan, Q4).
  • Q1’s shareholder-return messaging focused on process and optionality, including "we intend to renew our Normal Course Issuer Bid at the end of June" (CEO Whelan, Q1), while Q4 emphasized the dividend step-up as "the largest nominal dividend increase in company history" (CEO Whelan, Q4).
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s weather-driven Kearl performance and inventory optimization to Q1’s restructuring milestones, SCO premiums, and whether higher prices could change capital pacing.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We've seen significant volatility in commodity markets, driven by geopolitical events in the Middle East," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "During March a third-party regional gas supply outage required us to temporarily reduce production levels," said (CEO Whelan).
  • "Syncrude experienced unplanned downtime associated with Coker 8-3," said (CEO Whelan), who also noted, "the decision was made to postpone the planned second quarter turnaround work... until the summer."
  • "We continue to optimize around hydrogen availability," said (CEO Whelan) in discussing renewable diesel at Strathcona.

Final Takeaway

Management tied Q1 results to market volatility and operational execution, highlighting $940 million of net income, $1,239 million of operating cash flow, and $478 million of CapEx, while keeping strategy unchanged around organic growth and shareholder returns. The company reiterated production and growth priorities—"Kearl getting it to 300,000" and "Cold Lake getting it to 165,000"—alongside continued restructuring implementation and plans to "renew our Normal Course Issuer Bid" in late June, with Q&A concentrating on pacing of in situ projects, downstream optimization toward diesel/jet, and the path for additional buybacks depending on cash generation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Park Hotels raises 2026 RevPAR growth outlook to 0.5%-2.5% while targeting $587M-$617M adjusted EBITDA
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered better-than-expected performance in the first quarter with RevPAR increasing 5.5% year-over-year, excluding our Royal Palm South Beach Hotel,” Thomas Baltimore said (Chairman, President & CEO Thomas Baltimore), adding that results reflected “continued strength in leisure demand at our resort properties” and “healthy corporate group demand.”

  • Baltimore said the company “recently sold the 396-room Hilton Seattle Airport Hotel… for $18 million,” and that “total Non-Core asset sales for the year” reached “$31 million,” while reiterating, “We continue to make solid progress on the remaining 12 Non-Core hotels and remain firmly committed to materially reducing our Non-Core exposure by year-end” (Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore).

  • On Royal Palm South Beach, Baltimore said, “We remain on track to achieve our target completion date by early June,” and highlighted early booking signals: “The property securing $1.4 million of group business as of the end of the first quarter for 2027 at an average rate of $460” (Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore). He also reiterated the underwriting framework: “We expect returns on invested capital between 15% to 20%, with EBITDA projected to more than double from approximately $14 million to $28 million upon stabilization” (Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore).

  • Sean Dell’Orto said, “Total hotel revenues for the quarter were $591 million… hotel adjusted EBITDA was $152 million… adjusted EBITDA of $143 million and adjusted FFO per share of $0.45,” and added that “liquidity at the end of the first quarter was approximately $2 billion” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Sean Dell’Orto).

Outlook

  • Dell’Orto said, “For the year, with Q1’s outperformance we are increasing our RevPAR growth guidance… to a new range of 0.5% to 2.5% and adjusted EBITDA guidance… to a new range of $587 million to $617 million, while AFFO… to a new range of $1.74 to $1.90 per share” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

  • On Q2 setup, Dell’Orto said, “April RevPAR is expected to be flat, but up 3%, excluding Miami,” and “Overall, we expect Q2 RevPAR to come in around the midpoint of our guidance range with roughly a 100 basis point drag from Miami” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

  • Baltimore emphasized conservatism around major events: “We have included in our guidance… we have no contribution coming from Miami in that process at this time,” while adding, “We are cautiously optimistic that we should be open in time” for Miami matches (Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore).

Financial Results

  • Dell’Orto reported, “RevPAR exceeded $191,” “Total hotel revenues for the quarter were $591 million,” “hotel adjusted EBITDA was $152 million,” “adjusted EBITDA of $143 million,” and “adjusted FFO per share of $0.45” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

  • On Royal Palm near-term drag, Dell’Orto said, “we are forecasting a nearly $3 million loss for Q2,” tied to ramping staffing and rebuilding demand, but added, “expect the resort to ramp up quickly over the back half of the year” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

  • On refinancing impacts, Dell’Orto said, “these refinancings are expected to increase interest expense by approximately $28 million, with roughly $13 million reflected in our 2026 AFFO guidance” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

Q&A

  • Floris Van Dijkum, Ladenburg Thalmann: Asked about Non-Core sale progress and “pushback” on portfolio quality; Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore said remaining Non-Core includes “12 assets,” with “the remaining 9 assets account for about $41 million in EBITDA,” and added, “We’re not holding out for the last dollar.”

  • Van Dijkum, Ladenburg Thalmann: Asked about World Cup impact in Miami; Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore said, “we have no contribution coming from Miami in that process at this time,” and described a “target public occupancy TCO… mid-June.”

  • Bennett Rose, Citigroup: Asked why expenses moved up; COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto said Q1 outperformance was “occupancy based,” and “expense growth was a little more than expected as well,” and management said it is being carried through assumptions.

  • Peter (for Duane), Evercore ISI: Asked for Hawaii recovery “building blocks”; Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore said Japanese travelers are “about 3% of our business approximately,” versus “high teens… pre-pandemic,” and added, “We still… generated north of $140 million in EBITDA,” versus “about $185 million… coming off the pandemic.”

  • Aryeh Klein, BMO: Asked about oil/fuel risk to Hawaii; Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore said a prolonged shock could “have an impact on… air travel,” while adding, “you might see… people start to… onshore themselves… and… Hawaii could certainly benefit.”

  • Chris Darling, Green Street: Asked about Hilton Hawaiian Village RevPAR index; COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto said it is “tracking in that 95 to just around 100,” with a goal “back to… 110 to 115.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on disposition execution, cost inflation, and Hawaii sensitivity (e.g., Van Dijkum asked whether Park would “hold out for the last dollar,” and Klein pressed on fuel-driven airfare impacts).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive-detail oriented in Q&A, stressing execution and conservatism (e.g., “we have no contribution coming from Miami… at this time” and “we’re not holding out for the last dollar” from Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore).

  • Versus last quarter, management maintained a cautious macro posture but used more explicit upward revisions (e.g., “increasing our RevPAR growth guidance” and raising adjusted EBITDA/AFFO ranges), while analysts continued to concentrate on asset sales and Hawaii trajectory.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved higher quarter-over-quarter: Q4 2025 guidance of “flat to up 2%” RevPAR and “$580 million to $610 million” adjusted EBITDA shifted to “0.5% to 2.5%” and “$587 million to $617 million” in Q1 2026 (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

  • Asset-sale progress updated from the January Hilton Checkers sale to adding the Hilton Seattle Airport sale, with year-to-date Non-Core proceeds now stated as “$31 million” (Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore).

  • Debt-maturity planning advanced from “active discussions” for a Bonnet Creek mortgage in Q4 to “raise a $700 million floating rate delayed draw mortgage on Bonnet Creek… expected to close this week” in Q1, along with a stated annualized interest expense step-up of “approximately $28 million” (COO, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dell’Orto).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged demand sensitivity to geopolitics and energy, with Chairman, President & CEO Baltimore citing “growing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and their potential impact on consumer discretionary spending and business investment sentiment.”

  • Royal Palm reopening execution risk remains embedded in guidance conservatism, with Baltimore saying, “we have no contribution coming from Miami… at this time,” tied to “the complexity of the inspection and regulatory process.”

  • Non-Core disposition timing remains uncertain, with Baltimore describing the process as “a little lumpy and choppy,” while reiterating the intent to reduce the “overhang.”

Final Takeaway

Park’s management described Q1 outperformance driven by resorts and improving group trends, then raised full-year ranges for RevPAR growth, adjusted EBITDA, and AFFO per share while keeping Miami’s Royal Palm reopening and World Cup upside largely out of guidance. Leadership emphasized continued Non-Core asset dispositions, a defined debt-maturity refinancing plan (including a $700 million Bonnet Creek mortgage), and near-term earnings drag from Royal Palm’s ramp, alongside longer-term return targets tied to major renovations in Miami and Hawaii.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: RTX wins $441.6M PATRIOT GEM-T missile contract boost deal
data.description:
• RTX (RTX) received a $441.6M contract modification for procurement of PATRIOT GEM-T missiles supporting Operation Epic Fury.
  • The modification brings the total contract value to $441.6M, fully aligned to this award tranche.
  • The work will be executed in Chambersburg, Pennsylvania, with completion expected by Sept. 30, 2026.
  • The full $441.6M in FY26 special funds was obligated at award; the contract is being handled by Army Contracting Command.

data.title: IRadimed outlines 2026 revenue of $91M-$96M as 3870 pump ASP lift trends near 20%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IRadimed (IRMD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We once again have a very positive performance to announce with the first quarter 2026 revenue of $22 million, a 13% increase over the first quarter of 2025," said (Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Roger Susi), adding that results reflected "solid execution across our product lines" and that revenue was "substantially derived from the... 3860 pump system," alongside "growing revenue support for our Ferro-magnetic Detection System."

  • (CEO Susi) updated early 3870 pricing and configuration trends: "we had anticipated that the 3870 pump deal typically its ASP would increase by some 10% to 14%... However... initial quoting and actual orders are showing a lift closer to 20% of 3870 ASP." He also said, "we are seeing that a majority of this new business is for Quad, 4 pump systems rather than simply replacing the older 3860 dual-channel system."

  • (CEO Susi) framed the near-term 3860-to-3870 transition as a ramp rather than a sudden uplift: "it will not be a step-change, but rather a controlled ramp," with "declining revenue derived from the older 3860 pump system... offset positively by increasing revenue from the new 3870 system as we ramp up its production."

  • "For the 3 months ended March 31, 2026, revenue was $22 million," said (CFO & Corporate Secretary John Glenn), detailing mix and profitability: "Gross Profit for the quarter was $16.8 million with a Gross Margin of 77%" and "Net income was $5.8 million or $0.45 per diluted share on a GAAP basis."

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we expect Revenue of $20 million to $21 million, GAAP diluted earnings per share of $0.40 to $0.44, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $0.44 to $0.48," said (CFO Glenn).

  • "For the full year 2026, we reaffirm our guidance with a Revenue of $91 million to $96 million, GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1.90 to $2.05, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $2.06 to $2.21," (CFO Glenn) said.

  • Versus the prior quarter’s launch timeline language, (CEO Susi) emphasized earlier-stage commercial execution in the U.S.: "we did not launch our sales effort for 3870 until late January" and said Q2 will be constrained by shipments even as orders build: "Q2 Revenue will not fully reflect this high level of exciting order activity."

Financial Results

  • (CFO Glenn) reported segment contributions for Q1 2026, including "IV infusion pump systems contributed $7.7 million" and "Patient vital signs monitoring systems contributed $7.1 million," while "Disposable revenue was $4.9 million" and "Ferro-magnetic Detection System contributed $600,000."

  • (CFO Glenn) highlighted expense and cash flow items: "Total Operating Expenses for the quarter were $9.6 million" and "Cash flow from operations was $8.3 million for the quarter," adding "Non-GAAP free cash flow was $7.8 million... after capital expenditures of approximately $500,000."

  • (CFO Glenn) noted capital return and balance sheet position: "We ended the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $56.4 million" and "the company's Board of Directors declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.20 per share... payable on May 29, 2026."

Q&A

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked why customers are shifting to Quad configurations and whether it is widespread; (CEO Susi) replied, "more than half of these orders we've taken so far have been for this Quad systems," attributing it to customer reaction to the demonstrated setup: "we walk in and we're showing the Quad Stak..." and said order timing has been faster: "orders that we're getting in at this point have been rather quick."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked whether Quad orders include better per-pump pricing; (CEO Susi) said, "No," and provided pricing context: "the list price of the previous pump was $20,000" and "the Quad systems... each coming in at more than $100,000."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked what drives the plan to add another 1,000 replacement channels per year and whether greenfield is included; (CEO Susi) said, "I'm just talking taking 1,000 out of the installed-base" and added, "Greenfield will be extra," while also stating, "I don't see that we're going to have time to start calling on the greenfield for a while."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked about manufacturing and scaling; (CEO Susi) said, "we're trying to take it a bit cautiously," reiterating "130, 135 pumps for this quarter" and describing an intended cadence: "third quarter, we'll plan to double that up again" while emphasizing execution needs: "stabilizing the supply chain."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and oriented around upside capture and execution capacity, including: "How are you feeling from that standpoint?" and "What are the drivers to that?"

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and reinforced confidence around early demand, while tempering near-term revenue translation: (CEO Susi) said interest has been "very gratifying" and called the order pace "a frenzy right now," while also cautioning, "Q2 Revenue will not fully reflect" the order activity.

  • Compared with the previous quarter’s tone emphasizing pilot feedback and an April ramp, the current call used more definitive early-commercial language, including "a majority of this new business is for Quad" and "bookings with greater-than-expected ASP," while repeating near-term constraint framing around production ramp pacing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The 3870 narrative shifted from Q4 2025 expectations of ASP "increase 10% to 14%" and planned initial shipment timing to Q1 2026 evidence of early pricing and mix: (CEO Susi) said current quoting and orders show "a lift closer to 20%" and that "more than half" of early orders are Quad configurations.

  • Guidance framing remained centered on a second-half 2026 acceleration, but the current quarter added sharper language around Q2 revenue pressure from the 3860 decline and 3870 ramp: "keeps Q2 Revenue somewhat in check."

  • Analyst focus narrowed in Q1 2026 to 3870 ordering patterns and manufacturing ramp, versus Q4 2025 when questions also covered gross margin trajectory, disposables/services growth, inventory transition management, and international regulatory timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CEO Susi) described a key near-term operational constraint as production capacity and ramp discipline: "The sales team would like us to ship over 200, but we just can't do it," and said the company is prioritizing execution: "We're going to get them right."

  • (CEO Susi) flagged supply chain stabilization as part of the ramp risk: "it's a matter of ramping up the know-how and stabilizing the supply chain."

  • (CFO Glenn) described tax rate variability driven by timing: "The increase in the effective tax rate... was largely due to the timing of deductions tied to the windfall deduction for equity grants," adding, "we anticipate that the rate will trend down."

Final Takeaway

IRadimed management positioned the Q1 2026 quarter as steady performance led by pumps and monitoring, while portraying the 3870 launch as the primary stock-moving catalyst: early orders are showing an ASP lift closer to 20% and a higher-than-expected mix of Quad system purchases. Management simultaneously tempered near-term expectations by calling Q2 a controlled production ramp that will not fully reflect order momentum, while reaffirming full-year 2026 guidance and emphasizing supply chain stabilization and measured scaling as the operational priorities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Casella outlines 2026 guidance of $473M-$483M adjusted EBITDA following $150M in annualized 2026 acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Casella Waste Systems (CWST) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are very pleased with our performance in the first quarter and the strong start it provides for 2026," said "we combined disciplined positive pricing, steady core operations and meaningful acquisition activity to position the business for a strong year" (President, CEO & Director Ned Coletta).
  • "Revenues for the quarter were $457.3 million or up 9.6% year-over-year," and "solid waste pricing was up 5.1% overall" (President Coletta).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA increased 12.3% year-over-year," and the company "delivered 50 basis points of margin expansion in the quarter" (President Coletta).
  • "We have completed 4 acquisitions so far in 2026, representing approximately $150 million of annualized revenues," including "the Star Waste acquisition" that "adds approximately $100 million of annualized revenues" (President Coletta).
  • "We are on track to cut $5 million of operating costs in 2026 and another $10 million over the next 2 years" in the Mid-Atlantic integration (President Coletta).
  • "We are on track with our previously announced $15 million in targeted G&A savings over the next 3 years" (President Coletta).
  • "We continue to make great permitting progress" with "the Hakes permit expected by the third quarter of 2026" and "the Hyland permit expected by the first quarter of 2027"; at Hyland, Casella is "working to more than double the annual permit...from 460,000 tons a year to 1 million tons a year" (President Coletta).
  • "Revenues in the first quarter were $457.3 million, up $40.2 million or 9.6% year-over-year," with "$23.9 million from acquisitions" and "$16.2 million from same-store growth" (Executive VP & CFO Bradford Helgeson).

Outlook

  • "We updated financial guidance for 2026 to reflect acquisitions closed to date," raising revenue guidance to "$2.06 billion to $2.08 billion," adjusted EBITDA to "$473 million to $483 million," and adjusted free cash flow to "$200 million to $210 million" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "Guidance further assumes adjusted EBITDA margins of approximately 20%," and the revision "reflects approximately $120 million of new annualized revenue for 9 months of the year" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "We've not yet increased our guidance for the base business after the first quarter" and "we'll reevaluate guidance in future quarters" (Executive VP Helgeson).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted net income was $12.8 million...or $0.20 per diluted share" and "GAAP net income was lower by $0.7 million...on higher acquisition expenses and additional costs associated with the organics facility closure" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $62.3 million" and "adjusted free cash flow was $30.7 million" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "Capital expenditures were $50 million" and "DSO was 34 days" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "As of March 31, we had $1.16 billion of debt and $127 million of cash" with covenant leverage at "2.29x"; "on a pro forma basis" after April 1 acquisitions, leverage was "approximately 2.75x" (Executive VP Helgeson).

Q&A

  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: "should we be thinking about a period of outsized margin expansion over the next couple of years?" Executive VP Helgeson: "we do see an above-trend margin improvement opportunity over the next 2 to 3 years."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: "the closure on Ontario could work out to be EBITDA neutral"—"can you just talk about the moving pieces there?" President Coletta: "we plan to close the Ontario landfill on December 31, 2028," and "we should be pretty neutral during that period" while "on an operating income basis, we'll actually come out the other side with a benefit."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: "provide an update on where we are along the landfill gas program?" President Coletta: "we have 4 projects online today" and "we expect this year several million dollars of EBITDA"; "the Waga projects, in particular, appear to be operating very well" but "we're a little early to start calling the exact production levels."
  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: asked about Star Waste margins and integration. Executive VP Helgeson: "our assumption...is about 20% EBITDA margin" and management expects to "get those margins up materially over time." President Coletta: "This is not a fix it one."
  • Patrick Brown, Raymond James: asked about the increase in landfill pricing and Q2 margin setup. President Coletta said the company "didn't have enough management of pipeline" and cited a rail move that had been pressuring the market but is now "full." Executive VP Helgeson: "It's not crazy to think" Q2 could be "slightly down...year-over-year basis, up sequentially though."
  • James Schumm, TD Cowen: raised investor concern that "landfill pricing is going to be depressed longer term." President Coletta: pricing has "generally been mid-single digits" over the last decade, and "the more expensive solution...is to move it via rail."
  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: asked about CPI impacts. Executive VP Helgeson: "70%, 75% of our collection business is open market." President Coletta: "we're trying to be really dynamic" on pricing.
  • Harold Antor, Jefferies: asked why pricing can remain near current levels and about churn. Executive VP Helgeson said the company reports "a net number of rollbacks" and expects Mid-Atlantic pricing to be a "tailwind" post-integration; churn was not quantified in the transcript.
  • William Grippin, Barclays: asked about leverage capacity after Star. Executive VP Helgeson: "at 2.75x, we do have capacity" and "we have about $500 million of available liquidity."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative to skeptical on long-term landfill pricing durability and near-term margins, including concerns that pricing was "sub-5%" (James Schumm, TD Cowen) and that Q2 margins could be pressured (Patrick Brown, Raymond James).
  • Management tone slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks (President Coletta: "very pleased"), and confident but measured in Q&A, including "we do see an above-trend margin improvement opportunity" (Executive VP Helgeson) and "we're a little early to start calling the exact production levels" on RNG (President Coletta).
  • Versus last quarter’s CEO-transition framing, the current call emphasized operating execution, acquisition integration, and updated guidance; Q&A remained centered on Mid-Atlantic synergies, landfill pricing, and capital allocation in both quarters.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased in Q1 2026 to revenue of "$2.06 billion to $2.08 billion" and adjusted EBITDA of "$473 million to $483 million" (Executive VP Helgeson), compared with the prior quarter’s initial 2026 guidance of revenue "$1.97 billion to $1.99 billion" and adjusted EBITDA "$455 million to $465 million" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • Acquisition messaging shifted from pipeline discussion in Q4 2025 to closed-deal impact in Q1 2026, including "4 acquisitions so far in 2026" and Star Waste (President Coletta).
  • Landfill pricing discussion shifted from Q4 2025 commentary that rail moves had "put a little bit of a lid on pricing" (President Coletta, prior call) to Q1 2026 commentary that the rail move discussed is "full" and the company rebuilt its landfill sales leadership (President Coletta).

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather-related softness was cited as "slightly negative volumes mainly due to the challenging winter weather" (President Coletta).
  • Commodity price pressure persisted, with "average recycled commodity revenue per ton...down 22%" year-over-year, though management said the net revenue impact was "only about $1 million" due to fee structures (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • Acquisition margin dilution remained a near-term factor, as new acquisitions "diluted margins by 15 basis points" (Executive VP Helgeson), with integration and synergies framed as the mitigation.
  • RNG execution risk with third-party developers was described as "mixed results" and "far longer" timelines (President Coletta).

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a broad-based execution quarter, pairing solid waste pricing and base-business margin expansion with a step-up in acquisitions led by Star Waste, and then raised full-year 2026 guidance to reflect deals closed to date. Leadership reiterated multi-year cost and synergy targets in the Mid-Atlantic and G&A, while highlighting permitting milestones at Hakes and Hyland and framing Ontario’s planned 2028 closure as potentially EBITDA-neutral with improved operating income profile over time.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Origin Materials plans to sell technology, wind down operations
data.description:
Origin Materials (ORGN) down 25.5% post-market Friday after saying its board determined it is in the best interests of its shareholders to sell its PET cap technology and other remaining assets, followed by an orderly wind down of operations.

Origin (ORGN) said its board approved the dissolution and liquidation of the company, subject to shareholder approval, and will call a special shareholder meeting to seek approval of the plan of dissolution; upon filing of the certificate of dissolution, it intends to cease trading in its common stock and warrants.

As part of an imminent workforce reduction, members of the executive team including CEO John Bissell will leave the company, with CFO and COO Matt Plavan appointed as interim CEO.

The company said it will provide an estimate of any amount that may be distributed to shareholders, but the amount actually distributable "may vary substantially from any estimate provided by the company, based on a number of factors."

data.title: Both up 7%: RIOT Platform sets earnings tone—will MSTR follow? Full story
data.description:
Bitcoin-linked stocks RIOT Platforms (RIOT) and Strategy (MSTR) rallied nearly 7% on May 1 as Bitcoin (BTC-USD) climbed back towards the $77K level. 

But the bigger question here is—did Riot Platform's earnings just set the tone for what Strategy could deliver next?

Riot Platforms reported Q1 revenue of $167.2M, up 3.6% Y/Y and beating analyst estimates by nearly $37.2M. However, GAAP EPS came in at -$1.44 vs. -$0.83 expected, hurt by a $326.7M non-cash Bitcoin revaluation loss and $97.7M depreciation.

Even though operating strength stood out. CFO Jamie Chung highlighted $21M in power credits, helping lower its Bitcoin mining cost to $44.6K per asset.

The company mined 1,473 Bitcoins in Q1, down 3.7% Y/Y, while holdings fell 18% to 15,680 BTC. Still, the company's average operating hash rate rose 23% to 36.4 EH/s.

A key upside was diversification—Riot Platform's data center revenue hit $33.2M in Q1, while Advanced Micro Devices doubled its capacity at its Rockdale site to 50 MW, strengthening its AI story.

Analyst sentiment also remains strong. Seeking Alpha analysts rate Riot Platforms a “Buy” with a 4.00 score, while Wall Street analysts maintain a stronger 4.57 “Strong Buy” rating.

This optimism also lifted Strategy ahead of its May 5 Q1 earnings report. Analysts expect the firm to report Q1 EPS of -18.98 on revenue of $125.1M. 

However, estimate revisions remain weak, with 0 EPS upgrades vs. 2 downgrades and 3 revenue upgrades vs. 1 downgrade.

Wall Street still keeps faith in the story, with a strong buy rating of 4.73, but the setup is simple: Riot Platforms already showed how crypto stocks react to Bitcoin strength—now the market is waiting to see if Strategy can follow the same path or break from it.

data.title: Moderna expects up to 10% 2026 revenue growth and $4.5B-$5B year-end cash, while targeting ~$4.2B cash costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moderna (MRNA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Moderna described Q1 performance as driven by international execution, with CEO Stéphane Bancel saying, "In the first quarter, we grew year-over-year revenues significantly to $0.4 billion, driven primarily by execution of our long-term strategic partnership in -- with the U.K. government" and adding, "With a strong Q1, we are reiterating up to 10% growth in 2026."
  • Bancel highlighted new EU regulatory milestones and their timing, stating, "we achieved an important milestone with the approval of mCOMBRIAX in the European Union" and "We also secured approval for mNEXSPIKE in the European Union," adding, "These 2 new approved products in Europe will be important growth drivers in the EU in 2027."
  • CFO James Mock emphasized Q1 revenue mix and near-term revenue expectations, saying, "For the first quarter, total revenue was $400 million" and "For the second quarter, we are expecting revenue of between $50 million and $100 million."
  • Mock detailed the Arbutus/Genevant settlement mechanics and uncertainty on the additional payment, stating, "we will make a lump sum payment of $950 million in the third quarter of 2026" and "If, however, the Federal Circuit affirms liability under Section 1498, Moderna has agreed to make an additional payment of up to $1.3 billion."
  • President Stephen Hoge framed the multi-year plan around partnerships and 2027 EU opportunity, saying, "we expect up to 10% revenue growth driven by our long-term strategic partnerships in the United Kingdom, Canada and Australia, and supported by the continued growth of mNEXSPIKE" and describing Europe as "the large $1.8 billion annual European respiratory vaccines market."

Outlook

  • Mock reiterated the 2026 framework and key assumptions, stating, "we expect total revenue to grow up to 10% in 2026" and "this guidance assumes no revenue from our flu vaccine or mCOMBRIAX."
  • Mock updated cost of sales guidance due to litigation, saying, "Our cost of sales projection has increased from $0.9 billion to $1.8 billion, and now includes the $0.9 billion litigation settlement charge."
  • Mock maintained cost targets and year-end liquidity outlook, stating, "we are expecting total GAAP operating expenses of $4.9 billion and cash costs of $4.2 billion" and "we expect to end 2026 with between $4.5 billion to $5 billion of cash and investments."
  • Compared with the prior call’s U.S. flu posture, management’s language shifted from Q4’s "disappointment" around the FDA process to Q1’s U.S. timing clarity, with Bancel saying, "In the U.S., our seasonal flu vaccine mRNA-1010 was assigned a PDUFA date of August 5."

Financial results

  • Mock reported Q1 revenue and a cost-of-sales impact tied to the settlement, stating, "revenue was $400 million in the quarter" and "Cost of sales for the quarter was $955 million. This includes $878 million related to our previously disclosed litigation settlement."
  • Mock described operating expense reductions, saying, "R&D expenses for the quarter were $649 million" and "SG&A expenses for the quarter were $173 million."
  • Mock quantified GAAP and non-GAAP loss views and liquidity, stating, "Net loss for the quarter was $1.3 billion or $3.40 per share" and "Excluding this item, the net loss would have been $0.5 billion or $1.18 per share" and "We ended the first quarter with cash and investments of $7.5 billion."

Q&A

  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "you newly disclosed initiation of a Phase III study for Intismeran as monotherapy"; President Hoge: "we're looking at, therefore, INT as monotherapy as opposed to just surgery and watchful waiting for high-risk Stage I disease."
  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "is it possible this is a double order year?"; President Hoge: "a second campaign is planned for the fall, and so the third and fourth quarter of this year."
  • Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: "can you refine that at all" on melanoma interim timing; President Hoge: "we won't refine that guidance" and "There is not a built-in futility assessment."
  • Luca Issi, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: "walk us through why the legal team deemed the additional $1.3 billion charge on 1498 is not probable"; CFO Mock: "we're not really going to comment too much on the merits of the trial" and "perhaps late 2027, maybe into 2028 is where we think that this might be resolved."
  • Tyler Van Buren, TD Cowen, Research Division: "what constitutes success" in adjuvant melanoma; President Hoge: "we think the opportunity for benefit could be anywhere between that 0.5 that we saw in a number like 0.8."
  • Eliana Merle, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: "what your expectations are for timing for data from RCC and muscle invasive bladder cancer"; President Hoge: "it's hard to guide right now" and "we can't guide on when that timing would be."
  • Michael Yee, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked whether interim implies hazard ratio differences and RCC timing; President Hoge: "we definitely have reserved alpha for both of those" and "The DSMB will look at that result and then advise us."
  • Courtney Breen, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division: asked on INT revenue recognition and Stage I market; CFO Mock: "it will end up being that we deliver the product to Merck"; President Hoge: "almost 1/3, north of 30% of diagnoses are now earlier stage."
  • Geoffrey Meacham, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked other IO combinations and norovirus bar; President Hoge: "most of our focus is on the combination with the PD-1 and KEYTRUDA" and "we want to see a vaccine efficacy that's also meaningful and so north of 50%."
  • Cory Kasimov, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked OS necessity in melanoma; President Hoge: "RFS is a pretty good predictor."
  • Simon Baker, Rothschild & Co Redburn, Research Division: asked Q2 mix and phasing; CFO Mock: "This was largely, the majority is COVID still" and "Last year, we had $700 million of sales outside the United States."
  • Lin Tsai, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked BD beyond mRNA; CEO Bancel: "If we find the right opportunity to increase what we can do, we will, of course, execute" and "we don't have a pipeline problem like most companies in the industry."
  • Alexandria Hammond, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked EU combo commercialization; President Hoge: "we do not expect revenues in the year of approval" and "We do hope for a successful launch in '27."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-skeptical, pressing on regulatory/legal uncertainty and timing, including "why the legal team deemed the additional $1.3 billion charge on 1498 is not probable" (Luca Issi, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division) and repeated requests to narrow timing like "can you refine that at all" (Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley, Research Division).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded on specifics in Q&A, with President Hoge saying, "we won't refine that guidance" and CFO Mock saying, "we're not really going to comment too much on the merits of the trial."
  • Versus Q4, the flu-related tone moved from uncertainty to process clarity, shifting from Q4’s "disappointment" framing to Q1’s "PDUFA date of August 5" (CEO Bancel) and "we would describe that as a pretty normal course" (President Hoge).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest change from Q4 was the U.S. flu regulatory status moving from "FDA Refusal-to-File" (Q4, CEO Bancel) to "assigned a PDUFA date of August 5" (Q1, CEO Bancel), alongside EU approvals for mCOMBRIAX and mNEXSPIKE becoming concrete Q1 milestones.
  • Financial framing shifted to incorporate the Arbutus/Genevant settlement into 2026 guidance, with CFO Mock updating, "Our cost of sales projection has increased from $0.9 billion to $1.8 billion."
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s emphasis on flu RTF implications toward Q1 concentration on INT trial design/interims and the litigation timeline, including "late 2027, maybe into 2028" (CFO Mock).

Risks and concerns

  • Litigation remains a key uncertainty, with CFO Mock stating, "If... the Federal Circuit affirms liability under Section 1498, Moderna has agreed to make an additional payment of up to $1.3 billion" and adding, "We have concluded that a loss related to this pending Section 1498 proceeding is not probable."
  • COVID demand pressure remains embedded in guidance assumptions, with Mock saying, "Our 2026 revenue guidance factors in potential future declines in COVID vaccination rates."
  • Regulatory timing and market access execution in Europe were framed as gating items for combo uptake, with President Hoge saying market access is "pricing and reimbursement" and noting that in Europe "that takes a couple of years as a process."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 as an internationally driven revenue quarter anchored by U.K. partnership deliveries, reiterated "up to 10%" 2026 revenue growth and a ~$4.2B adjusted cash cost objective, and positioned EU approvals of mCOMBRIAX and mNEXSPIKE as 2027 growth drivers. The call also centered investor attention on the Arbutus/Genevant settlement’s cash timing and the Section 1498 appeal path, while Q&A concentrated on the expanding Intismeran Phase III program design and event-driven readout timing across oncology and norovirus.
